content:
The **softmax function** emerged from **statistical mechanics** (Boltzmann distribution over energy states) and was adopted in **logistic regression** and **multinomial models** as the natural way to map linear scores to **Categorical probabilities**.

In the **1980s–1990s** neural network renaissance, the **softmax output layer** became standard for multi-class classification alongside cross-entropy loss — the same pairing used in modern deep learning.

**Numerical analysis** contributed the **log-sum-exp trick**, making stable computation of log probabilities routine in libraries from LAPACK-era code through NumPy, PyTorch, and TensorFlow.

Today softmax appears at every scale: from 10-class MNIST heads to **50k+ vocabulary** language models, always converting logits to a valid probability simplex before argmax decisions or cross-entropy training.

timeline.label: Boltzmann / statistical mechanics
timeline.description: Exponential normalization over states — conceptual ancestor of softmax as a Gibbs distribution over discrete outcomes.

timeline.label: Multinomial logistic regression
timeline.description: Linear models extend binary logistic regression to K classes via softmax over class logits.

timeline.label: Deep learning (1980s–2000s)
timeline.description: Neural classifiers standardize linear → softmax → cross-entropy as the default multi-class head.

timeline.label: Stable log-sum-exp in ML frameworks
timeline.description: Production libraries implement log_softmax and fused cross-entropy using max-subtraction for numerical robustness.
